regions.name: Primary Motor Cortex
regions.fun_fact: Stimulating the mouse primary motor cortex triggers specific whisker and limb movements — researchers use this to map movement circuits.

regions.name: Secondary Motor Cortex
regions.fun_fact: MOs is involved in planning and preparing movements before they happen.

regions.name: Primary Somatosensory Cortex, Barrel Field
regions.fun_fact: Each 'barrel' in this cortex corresponds one-to-one with a single whisker. Mice navigate the world largely through whisking.

regions.name: Primary Somatosensory Cortex
regions.fun_fact: The somatosensory cortex contains a 'mouse homunculus' — a map of the body surface across the cortex.

regions.name: Primary Visual Cortex
regions.fun_fact: Much of what we know about visual cortex organization comes from studies in mice — they have a large visual cortex relative to brain size.

regions.name: Lateral Visual Cortex
regions.fun_fact: VISl is part of the mouse's higher visual hierarchy, processing more complex features than V1.

regions.name: Primary Auditory Cortex
regions.fun_fact: Mice can hear ultrasonic vocalizations (USVs) up to 100 kHz — their auditory cortex is specialized for this range.

regions.name: Retrosplenial Cortex
regions.fun_fact: RSP is crucial for spatial navigation and memory consolidation — the mouse 'GPS system'.

regions.name: Prefrontal Cortex
regions.fun_fact: Mice use PFC for working memory and decision-making, though it's proportionally smaller than in primates.

regions.name: Anterior Cingulate Cortex
regions.fun_fact: ACA is involved in social behavior, pain processing, and conflict monitoring in mice.

regions.name: Hippocampus
regions.fun_fact: Place cells in the mouse hippocampus fire when the animal is in a specific location — the cellular basis of the cognitive map.

regions.name: Hippocampal Field CA1
regions.fun_fact: CA1 pyramidal neurons are among the most-studied neurons in neuroscience — thousands of papers have examined their role in memory.

regions.name: Hippocampal Field CA3
regions.fun_fact: CA3 is thought to act as a pattern completer — filling in missing information from partial cues.

regions.name: Dentate Gyrus
regions.fun_fact: The dentate gyrus is one of the rare brain regions that generates new neurons (neurogenesis) throughout life in mice.

regions.name: Amygdala
regions.fun_fact: Mice use their amygdala to form fear memories in a single trial — a single shock is enough to create a lasting association.

regions.name: Basolateral Amygdala
regions.fun_fact: BLA neurons form direct connections with prefrontal cortex — disrupting this pathway impairs fear extinction.

regions.name: Thalamus
regions.fun_fact: Almost all sensory information passes through the thalamus before reaching the cortex — it's the brain's relay station.

regions.name: Dorsal Lateral Geniculate Nucleus
regions.fun_fact: LGd is the visual thalamic relay — retinal ganglion cells project here before information reaches V1.

regions.name: Medial Geniculate Nucleus
regions.fun_fact: MGN is the auditory thalamic relay — and is also a key site for fear conditioning to auditory cues.

regions.name: Striatum
regions.fun_fact: The striatum is the main input structure of the basal ganglia, receiving dopamine signals that encode reward prediction errors.

regions.name: Caudoputamen
regions.fun_fact: In mice, the caudate and putamen are fused into one structure (caudoputamen), unlike in primates.

regions.name: Nucleus Accumbens
regions.fun_fact: The nucleus accumbens is the brain's 'reward hub' — it's strongly activated by food, sex, social interaction, and addictive drugs.

regions.name: Globus Pallidus, External
regions.fun_fact: GPe contains the 'indirect pathway' neurons that suppress unwanted movements.

regions.name: Substantia Nigra, Reticular Part
regions.fun_fact: SNr provides tonic inhibition to the thalamus — when released, movement is allowed to occur.

regions.name: Substantia Nigra, Compact Part
regions.fun_fact: SNc contains the dopaminergic neurons lost in Parkinson's disease — mice are used extensively to model this disorder.

regions.name: Ventral Tegmental Area
regions.fun_fact: VTA dopamine neurons fire when something unexpected and rewarding happens — they encode 'reward prediction error'.

regions.name: Hypothalamus
regions.fun_fact: Specific hypothalamic neurons control hunger, thirst, fear, aggression, and sex — a tiny structure with enormous power.

regions.name: Lateral Hypothalamus
regions.fun_fact: Stimulating the lateral hypothalamus causes insatiable eating — lesioning it causes starvation, even with food available.

regions.name: Cerebellum
regions.fun_fact: The mouse cerebellum contains more neurons than the rest of the brain combined — mostly granule cells.

regions.name: Cerebellar Nuclei
regions.fun_fact: The deep cerebellar nuclei are the only output of the cerebellum — Purkinje cells in the cortex inhibit them.

regions.name: Medulla
regions.fun_fact: The medulla controls breathing, heart rate, and blood pressure — damage here is rapidly fatal.

regions.name: Pons
regions.fun_fact: The pons contains circuits for REM sleep — mice show characteristic pons-initiated sleep oscillations.

regions.name: Midbrain
regions.fun_fact: The mouse midbrain contains the superior colliculus — a region that detects overhead threats and triggers escape responses.

regions.name: Superior Colliculus
regions.fun_fact: A single neuron in the mouse superior colliculus can trigger a full predator escape response when activated.

regions.name: Inferior Colliculus
regions.fun_fact: The inferior colliculus is an obligatory relay for nearly all ascending auditory information.

regions.name: Locus Coeruleus
regions.fun_fact: There are only ~1,500 locus coeruleus neurons in the mouse brain, yet they project norepinephrine throughout the entire cortex.

regions.name: Dorsal Raphe Nucleus
regions.fun_fact: The dorsal raphe is the primary source of serotonin in the brain — most antidepressants target its serotonin release.

regions.name: Olfactory Bulb
regions.fun_fact: The olfactory bulb is proportionally enormous in mice — smell is their dominant sense. Mice can identify thousands of odors.

regions.name: Piriform Cortex
regions.fun_fact: Piriform cortex is a paleocortex — one of the evolutionarily oldest parts of the mammalian cortex.

regions.name: Entorhinal Cortex
regions.fun_fact: Grid cells in the entorhinal cortex create a spatial coordinate system — like a GPS grid overlaid on the world.

regions.name: Corpus Callosum
regions.fun_fact: The corpus callosum contains ~20 million axons in mice connecting the two hemispheres.
